title: King's Indian Defense: Four Pawns Attack
summary: Play the aggressive Four Pawns Attack against the King's Indian, building a large center for a direct assault.
description: The Four Pawns Attack is White's most aggressive try against the King's Indian Defense. By pushing e4, e5, and then building a pawn center, White challenges Black's position directly. This tutorial covers the main line and typical tactics.
line: 1.d4 Nf6 2.c4 g6 3.Nc3 Bg7 4.e4 d6 5.f4 O-O 6.Nf3 c5 7.d5 e6

variations.mainLine: 7...e6 - The classical Four Pawns with pawns on e4, e5, f4
variations.modernLine: 7...Na6 - The Modern Four Pawns with early queenside expansion
variations.avoidance: 4.Nf3 - Avoiding the KID with the Trompowsky

objectives: Understand the Four Pawns structure and why White builds a large center
Learn the typical plans: push e5 to attack, or play positionally
Recognize when to attack and when to consolidate
plans: White's plan: Build a big center, then push e5 to attack Black's king
Black's plan: Play ...f5 to break the center, or ...c4 for queenside play
The move ...Na6 is a common alternative, avoiding the main lines
Both sides must be careful about the e5/e6 tension in the center
commonMistakes: Do not push e5 too early - wait for proper development
Avoid weakening your king by pushing too many pawns forward
Do not underestimate Black's counterattack - the KID is dangerous
White should maintain central control before launching the final attack
completionTitle: Complex position with attacking chances for White
completionSummary: The Four Pawns Attack is a sharp, aggressive line. You've learned the main ideas from both sides.

steps.title: Claim central space
steps.instruction: Play d4 to control the center.
steps.coaching: The King's Indian is one of Black's most dynamic defenses.
steps.hint: Push the queen pawn two squares.

steps.title: Black develops the knight
steps.coaching: Black attacks d4 and prepares the King's Indian setup.

steps.title: Challenge the center
steps.coaching: This is the standard setup against the King's Indian.

steps.title: Black plays the King's Indian
steps.instruction: Black plays g6, setting up the fianchetto.
steps.coaching: This is the King's Indian! Black will develop the bishop to g7.

steps.title: Develop the knight
steps.instruction: Move Nc3, developing toward the center.
steps.coaching: White develops naturally, controlling d5 and e4.

steps.title: The King's Indian bishop
steps.coaching: The dragon bishop controls the long diagonal.

steps.title: Begin the Four Pawns
steps.instruction: Play e4 to build a large center.
steps.coaching: This is the Four Pawns Attack! White builds a central pawn mass.

steps.title: Black supports the center
steps.instruction: The pawn on d6 supports e5.
steps.coaching: Black prepares the typical King's Indian structure.

steps.title: Extend the center
steps.instruction: Play f4 to strengthen the e4 pawn.
steps.coaching: White builds a pawn center: e4, f4. Very aggressive!

steps.title: Black castles
steps.coaching: Both sides are now castled. The battle for the center intensifies.

steps.title: Complete development
steps.coaching: White develops the knight, supporting e5.

steps.title: Black challenges the center
steps.coaching: This is Black's typical break to challenge White's center.

steps.title: White advances in the center
steps.instruction: Play d5 to create a large pawn center.
steps.coaching: This is the main line! White creates a massive pawn center.

steps.title: Black pushes in the center
steps.coaching: Black meets the center advance with e6, creating tension.

steps.title: The key advance
steps.instruction: Play e5 to attack Black's king.
steps.coaching: This is the key move! White attacks with the pawn center.

steps.title: Black breaks the center
steps.instruction: Play f5 to challenge the center.
steps.coaching: This is Black's typical break against the Four Pawns.
